era_context: Late Fourth Dynasty Egypt; Early Dynastic Period III Mesopotamia. The era of the great pyramid-building pharaohs is entering its final phase. Monumental construction remains a defining feature of Egyptian state power. Mesopotamian city-states continue their competitive dynamics.

events.title: Reign of Shepseskaf, Fourth Dynasty Egypt
events.description: Shepseskaf is traditionally dated to reign during this period as the sixth or seventh pharaoh of the Fourth Dynasty, likely the successor of Menkaure. His reign marks a transition in Egyptian monumental architecture; rather than constructing a pyramid, he commissioned a mastaba-style tomb (the Mastaba of Shepseskaf at Saqqara). This shift may reflect changing religious beliefs, economic constraints, or political priorities. His reign is conventionally dated circa 2503–2498 BCE.
key_figures: Userkaf (possible successor, founder of Fifth Dynasty)
events.certainty_note: Conventional dating places Shepseskaf's reign circa 2503–2498 BCE. Chronological uncertainties of ±50 years apply. The shift from pyramid to mastaba construction is archaeologically attested, but the reasons for this shift are debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional chronology of Shepseskaf's reign is subject to the same uncertainties as his predecessors. The reasons for the shift from pyramid to mastaba construction are debated; some scholars attribute this to economic decline, others to religious or political change. The length of his reign (conventionally 4–6 years) is uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -3022 is sparse. The year is defined by the conventional chronology of Egyptian royal succession. The shift in monumental architecture is archaeologically attested and represents a significant cultural change, but the specific events of this year are not documented. Mesopotamian sources are absent.

graph_edges.note: Shepseskaf's reign ends the Fourth Dynasty; the Fifth Dynasty begins
